---
pagetitle: "Guidance or Misdirection? Unpacking the Role of Feedback in ... | Publications | Mesfin Genie"
description: "In stated-preference studies, researchers sometimes give respondents feedback about their earlier answers. This paper asks whether that feedback impro"
title-block-style: none
page-layout: full
image: /assets/og-card.png
---

```{=html}
<nav class="breadcrumb" aria-label="Breadcrumb"><a href="/">Home</a> / <a href="/publications/">Publications</a> / <span aria-current="page">Guidance or Misdirection? Unpacking the ...</span></nav>
<header class="page-header"><div class="page-shell">
<p class="eyebrow">Publication · Health Economics · 2026</p>
<h1>Guidance or Misdirection? Unpacking the Role of Feedback in Health Preference Assessments</h1>
</div></header>

<section class="content-section"><div class="page-shell narrow">
<p class="citation"><strong>Mesfin G. Genie; Shelby D. Reed; Semra Ozdemir</strong> (2026). Guidance or Misdirection? Unpacking the Role of Feedback in Health Preference Assessments. <em>Health Economics</em>. <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/hec.70093" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">doi:10.1002/hec.70093</a></p>

<h2>In plain language</h2>
<p>In stated-preference studies, researchers sometimes give respondents feedback about their earlier answers. This paper asks whether that feedback improves the quality of the data or quietly changes the preferences being measured.</p>

<h2>Research question</h2>
<p>Does giving respondents feedback during a health-preference task help them answer better, or steer them towards a particular pattern of answers?</p>

<h2>Approach</h2>
<p>A health-preference assessment examining the role of feedback in how respondents complete preference tasks.</p>

<h2>Contribution</h2>
<p>It clarifies when feedback supports genuine preferences and when it risks manufacturing consistency, which matters for how preference studies are designed and reported.</p>

<h2>Why it matters</h2>
<p>It informs good practice in preference elicitation, including whether and how to use feedback and how to report it.</p>

<h2>Read the paper</h2>
<p>The full methods, results, and limitations are reported in the published article. Please read and cite the original.</p>
<p><a class="button-primary" href="https://doi.org/10.1002/hec.70093" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">View at publisher (DOI)</a></p>

<h2>Related resources</h2>
<ul class="cv-list">
<li><a href="/blog/posts/does-feedback-help-people-choose/">Does feedback help people make better choices?</a></li>
<li><a href="/research/projects/health-preferences-dce/">Health preferences and discrete choice research</a></li>
</ul>
</div></section>
<script type="application/ld+json">{"@context": "https://schema.org", "@type": "ScholarlyArticle", "headline": "Guidance or Misdirection? Unpacking the Role of Feedback in Health Preference Assessments", "author": [{"@type": "Person", "name": "Mesfin G. Genie"}, {"@type": "Person", "name": "Shelby D. Reed"}, {"@type": "Person", "name": "Semra Ozdemir"}], "datePublished": "2026", "isPartOf": {"@type": "Periodical", "name": "Health Economics"}, "sameAs": "https://doi.org/10.1002/hec.70093", "url": "https://doi.org/10.1002/hec.70093"}</script>
```
